summary: Martin Fowler reflects on Fred Brooks's 1975 classic The Mythical Man-Month, noting that while some content feels dated in 2026, many lessons remain relevant. He highlights Brooks's Law about the dangers of adding people to late projects due to exponential communication overhead. Fowler emphasizes that his most enduring takeaway is Brooks's concept of conceptual integrity — the idea that a coherent, unified design trumps a collection of good but uncoordinated ideas. He recommends the anniversary edition for its inclusion of the influential 'No Silver Bullet' essay.
key_points: Brooks's Law warns that adding people to a late project increases communication paths exponentially, making it even later
Conceptual integrity — a unified, coherent design — is more valuable than accumulating many good but independent ideas
Conceptual integrity arises from both simplicity and straightforwardness, where straightforwardness means ease of composing elements
Fowler credits this concept as a foundational influence on his entire career and body of work
money_quotes: Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later.
As the number of people grows, the number of communication paths between those people grows exponentially.
Perhaps my most enduring lesson from this book is the importance of conceptual integrity.

It is better to have a system omit certain anomalous features and improvements, but to reflect one set of design ideas, than to have one that contains many good but independent and uncoordinated ideas.
He argues that conceptual integrity comes from both simplicity and straightforwardness - the latter being how easily we can compose elements.
The pursuit of conceptual integrity underpins much of my work.
key_insight: Conceptual integrity — a unified, coherent design philosophy — matters more than accumulating individually good but uncoordinated features, a lesson from 1975 that remains essential today.
